homepage.title: Hero promotes app download instead of products/offers
homepage.header: A product-focused hero with clear CTA can increase homepage engagement by 20-30% — Robo Craze's hero promotes app download instead of showcasing products or offers
observations: The hero carousel rotates between slides promoting app download ('Redeem RC Coins on the App'), coding kits, and Raspberry Pi — none lead with a compelling product offer or value proposition
The first fold is dominated by two persistent banners: 'Download our App' sticky bar and 'Save more when you Buy more' announcement — together consuming ~120px of precious mobile real estate
No trust signals visible above the fold — India's electronics buyers need reassurance about authorized products, warranty, and genuine components before browsing
Competitor ThinkRobotics leads with product imagery and 'Explore Products' CTA, while The Pi Hut shows product range with '8,300+ products' badge immediately
recommendations: Replace app-download hero with a product-focused hero: lead product (e.g., Raspberry Pi 5), headline emphasizing 'India's #1 Robotics Store', and a clear 'Shop Now' CTA
Add a trust/value strip below header: 'Authorized Reseller | Same Day Shipping | COD Available | 50K+ Makers Trust Us' — converts browsing visitors to buyers
Move app download promotion to a less prominent position (footer, post-purchase, or in-cart upsell)
homepage.benchmark_tag: Standard — 10/10 electronics stores lead with product imagery in hero

homepage.title: Spin-to-win popup blocks content on first visit
homepage.header: Aggressive popups on first visit increase bounce rate by 10-15% — Robo Craze shows a full-screen spin wheel immediately on mobile
observations: A full-screen 'Spin to Win' popup from Bitespeed appears within 3-5 seconds of landing, completely blocking the homepage content
The popup requires phone number (+91) and email before any interaction — high-friction data capture on first visit when user hasn't seen any products yet
The Pi Hut and Pimoroni show zero popups on first visit, letting users browse products immediately — popup-free first impressions build trust in electronics retail
Google's Page Experience guidelines penalize sites with intrusive interstitials, especially on mobile — this directly impacts search rankings
recommendations: Delay popup by 60+ seconds or trigger on exit-intent only — let users discover products first before asking for contact info
Replace full-screen spin wheel with a subtle bottom banner offering 5% off on first order — less intrusive, same capture goal
Show the popup only to returning visitors who haven't purchased — first-time visitors need product discovery, not gamification
homepage.benchmark_tag: Growing — 3/10 electronics stores use gamified popups; 7/10 use no or minimal popups

homepage.title: Broken collection sections on homepage
homepage.header: Empty product sections destroy homepage credibility — Robo Craze shows 'Sorry, there are no products in this collection' for multiple homepage sections
observations: Both 'STEM Kits' and 'Tools & Instruments' sections on the homepage display 'Sorry, there are no products in this collection' — visible to every visitor scrolling through
These broken sections suggest either misconfigured collection handles, empty collections, or sync issues between theme sections and Shopify collections
Two consecutive broken sections create a perception that the store is poorly maintained or has inventory issues — critical for a technical/electronics retailer where trust is paramount
ThinkRobotics and The Pi Hut curate homepage sections with hand-picked products, ensuring every section is populated and drives clicks
recommendations: Immediately fix the collection handles for STEM Kits and Tools & Instruments sections — verify the collection slugs match the theme section configuration
Implement conditional rendering: if a collection has 0 products, hide the entire section automatically instead of showing an error message
Regularly audit homepage sections monthly to catch empty collections before they impact customer experience
homepage.benchmark_tag: Standard — 10/10 stores have fully populated homepage sections

homepage.title: No star ratings or social proof on homepage product cards
homepage.header: Displaying review ratings on product cards increases click-through by 15-20% — Robo Craze's homepage products show no star ratings
homepage.benchmark_label: The Pi Hut — Product Cards with Ratings
observations: Homepage product cards show product name, MRP strikethrough, sale price, and savings amount — but no star ratings or review counts despite Judge.me being installed
The Pi Hut displays star ratings and review counts (e.g., '572 Reviews') directly on product cards, helping users make faster decisions and building trust
For electronics/components where products look similar in thumbnails, star ratings become a key differentiator that helps users identify popular and trusted products
Judge.me is already installed on Robo Craze — the star ratings just need to be surfaced on collection/homepage product cards
recommendations: Enable Judge.me star rating badges on all product card templates — this is typically a theme setting or snippet addition, not a new app installation
Show both star rating and review count (e.g., '4.5★ (29)') to combine social proof with specificity — bare stars without count look less credible
homepage.benchmark_tag: Standard — 8/10 electronics stores show ratings on product cards

homepage.title: Product images slow to load in homepage grid
homepage.header: Slow-loading product images increase homepage bounce rate — several category and product thumbnails on Robo Craze's homepage render as blank placeholders
observations: Multiple product thumbnails in the homepage grid render as blank/empty containers before images load — visible during scroll on both mobile and desktop
The product grid section for 3D Printers and featured products shows images that fail to load or load with significant delay, creating empty white boxes
The Pi Hut's collection pages load product images instantly with proper lazy-loading and optimized image sizes for each viewport breakpoint
Slow image loading in the electronics category is especially damaging because product differentiation relies heavily on visual identification of components
recommendations: Implement proper lazy-loading with low-quality image placeholders (LQIP) — show a blurred thumbnail while the full image loads to eliminate blank containers
Optimize all product images to WebP format at appropriate sizes: 300px for mobile cards, 600px for desktop cards — Shopify CDN supports automatic WebP conversion
homepage.benchmark_tag: Standard — 9/10 stores have instantly-loading product images

collection.title: No star ratings on collection product cards
collection.header: Star ratings on collection pages can boost product click-through rates by 15-25% — Robo Craze's collection cards show no ratings despite having Judge.me installed
collection.benchmark_label: The Pi Hut — Collection with Ratings
observations: Collection page product cards display product name, MRP strikethrough, sale price, savings badge, and ATC button — but zero star ratings or review counts
The Pi Hut shows star ratings and review counts on every collection product card, helping users quickly identify popular and trusted products
With 5,000+ products across similar-looking electronic components, star ratings serve as a critical navigation aid — they help users choose between multiple similar options
Judge.me is installed and reviews exist (29 reviews on Raspberry Pi 4) — the data is available, just not displayed on collection cards
recommendations: Enable Judge.me collection badge display — add the jdgm-widget snippet to the product card template in the Ella theme's collection page
Prioritize products with higher review counts in default sort to showcase social proof and encourage reviews on newer products
collection.benchmark_tag: Standard — 8/10 electronics stores display ratings on collection product cards

collection.title: No advanced filtering by specs or price range
collection.header: Technical product stores need specification-based filtering — Robo Craze offers only basic category navigation without price range, brand, or spec filters
observations: The collection page shows a basic 'Filter' button and a left sidebar with category tree navigation only — no price range slider, no brand filter, no specification-based filters
Electronics buyers frequently filter by: price range (budget vs premium), brand (Raspberry Pi vs Arduino), connectivity (WiFi, Bluetooth), RAM size, processor type — none available
ThinkRobotics offers sidebar filters with brand, price range, and product attributes that let technical buyers narrow down from hundreds of similar components quickly
With Robo Craze's catalog of 5,000+ products, basic category navigation forces users into excessive scrolling and page-by-page browsing
recommendations: Implement a filter app like Boost Product Filter & Search ($19-39/mo) that supports: price range slider, brand checkboxes, and tag-based specification filters
Add key specification tags to products (e.g., 'WiFi', 'Bluetooth 5.0', '8GB RAM', '4-Core') and surface them as filterable attributes — critical for technical product discovery
collection.benchmark_tag: Standard — 9/10 electronics stores offer specification-based filtering

collection.title: No quick-view modal on collection pages
collection.header: Quick-view functionality reduces navigation friction and can increase add-to-cart rate by 10-15% — Robo Craze requires full page navigation for every product
collection.client_label: Robo Craze — Mobile Collection (No Quick View)
observations: To check product specs or variants, users must navigate away from the collection page to the full PDP — then use the back button to return to browsing
For component shoppers comparing 5-10 similar items (e.g., different Raspberry Pi models), full page navigation for each creates significant friction
The Ella theme supports quick-view modals natively — this feature appears to be disabled in Robo Craze's configuration
A quick-view showing key specs, price, and ATC button lets users compare products without losing their place in the collection
recommendations: Enable the Ella theme's built-in quick-view modal — this shows product image, title, price, key details, and ATC button in an overlay without full page navigation
Ensure the quick-view modal includes the key spec bullet points and variant selector (if applicable) to enable informed decisions without leaving the collection
collection.benchmark_tag: Growing — 5/10 electronics stores offer quick-view on collection pages

collection.title: Sale percentage badges dominate product cards
collection.header: Overemphasizing discount percentages (11%, 55%, 49%, 62%) over product features can erode brand trust — electronics buyers prioritize specs over deals
collection.benchmark_label: The Pi Hut — Clean Product Presentation
observations: Every product card prominently displays a red 'Sale XX%' badge — with percentages ranging from 11% to 62%, making the collection feel like a perpetual clearance sale
The Pi Hut presents products with clean imagery, star ratings, and clear pricing without aggressive discount badges — building a premium electronics retailer perception
Electronics buyers are spec-driven, not deal-driven — a '55% off' badge on an HDMI adapter raises questions about product quality rather than creating urgency
Excessive MRP inflation (55-62% discounts) is flagged as an anti-pattern in electronics benchmarks — it erodes trust with technically savvy buyers
recommendations: Replace percentage-based sale badges with value-added badges like 'Bestseller', 'New Arrival', or 'In Stock' — these communicate value without discount dependency
Keep the savings amount in the price section (Save Rs. 1,643) but remove the prominent % badge from the product card image overlay
collection.benchmark_tag: Differentiator — 3/10 electronics stores avoid prominent discount percentage badges

pdp.title: No key spec icons or badges on PDP
pdp.header: Visual spec icons help electronics buyers make 40% faster purchase decisions — Robo Craze buries technical specs in a wall of text
observations: Product specifications are presented as plain bullet points buried below the fold — 'High-Performance Computing: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B with 8GB RAM for efficient and versatile computing'
Key specs like RAM (8GB), processor speed (1.5GHz), connectivity (WiFi 5, BT 5.0), and ports (USB 3.0, Dual 4K HDMI) are hidden in paragraph text instead of scannable visual icons
Electronics benchmark leaders like boAt use visual spec strips (e.g., '50H playtime, 13mm drivers, ENx Mic, BT 5.3') as scannable icon rows directly below the product title
Technical buyers scanning multiple products need instant spec comparison — text paragraphs force them to read entire descriptions to extract basic specifications
recommendations: Add a visual spec icon strip below the product title showing 6-8 key specs: RAM, CPU, WiFi, Bluetooth, USB ports, HDMI, GPIO pins — using icons + short labels
Move detailed descriptions to a 'Full Specifications' tab below the fold, keeping the purchase decision area focused on scannable specs and trust signals
pdp.benchmark_tag: Standard — 9/10 electronics stores show key specs as visual icons

pdp.title: No delivery/pincode checker on PDP
pdp.header: A delivery date estimator with pincode check can boost conversion by 5-8% — Robo Craze shows no delivery timeline or availability check on product pages
observations: No pincode-based delivery checker is present on the PDP — users cannot check delivery availability, estimated delivery date, or shipping charges before adding to cart
Robo Craze already mentions 'Same Day Shipping' and 'In-Store Pickup (Bangalore)' in trust badges — but these are generic claims, not personalized to the user's pincode
For electronics components needed for projects, delivery timeline is a critical purchase factor — a maker with a weekend project needs to know if the part arrives by Saturday
The store already has in-store pickup capability (Bangalore) — this should be surfaced on the PDP with pincode-based availability, not hidden until the cart page
recommendations: Add a pincode-based delivery checker showing: estimated delivery date, express vs standard options, store pickup availability (Bangalore), and COD eligibility for the entered pincode
Auto-detect returning users' pincode from previous orders or browser geolocation to pre-fill the delivery estimate — reducing friction for repeat buyers
pdp.benchmark_tag: Growing — 7/10 Indian electronics stores show delivery estimates on PDP

pdp.title: No EMI/installment option for high-value products
pdp.header: EMI options can increase conversion by 15-20% on products above Rs. 1,000 — Robo Craze's Rs. 12,856 Raspberry Pi shows no EMI or BNPL option
observations: A Raspberry Pi 4 8GB at Rs. 12,856 and other products like 3D printers (up to Rs. 4,39,999) and drones show no EMI, Snapmint, or BNPL (Buy Now Pay Later) payment options
Croma, the benchmark leader for Indian electronics, shows a full EMI calculator with monthly breakdowns and bank-specific offers — this is standard for Indian electronics above Rs. 1,000
For Robo Craze's target audience of students, hobbyists, and educational institutions, EMI makes higher-value products (kits, printers, boards) accessible without full upfront payment
GoKwik is detected as the checkout provider — Snapmint or Simpl BNPL integration can be added alongside GoKwik for pre-checkout payment visibility
recommendations: Integrate Snapmint or Simpl for BNPL/EMI display on PDP — show 'Pay in 3 installments of Rs. X/month' directly below the price for products above Rs. 999
For high-value items (Rs. 10,000+), show a full EMI calculator with bank-specific no-cost EMI options — this is critical for 3D printers, robotics kits, and premium boards
pdp.benchmark_tag: Differentiator — 4/10 electronics stores show EMI options on PDP (5/5 Indian stores)

pdp.title: Promotional banner takes valuable PDP real estate
pdp.header: PDP space above the fold directly impacts conversion — Robo Craze's 'Savings in Bulk' banner pushes product details below the fold on mobile
pdp.benchmark_label: The Pi Hut — Clean PDP First Fold
observations: A full-width 'SAVINGS IN BULK — UPTO OFF 30%' banner is displayed at the top of every PDP page, consuming approximately 60px of mobile viewport — pushing product details further below the fold
Combined with the app download banner (40px) and announcement bar (40px), nearly 140px of the mobile first fold is occupied by non-product promotional content
The Pi Hut's PDP immediately shows: product image, title, star ratings (1120 reviews), variant selector, price, and ATC button — all within the first fold
Bulk ordering is a B2B use case relevant to a small percentage of visitors — it should not occupy prime PDP real estate that every visitor sees
recommendations: Remove the 'Savings in Bulk' banner from PDP pages — the 'Bulk Order?' section already exists lower on the page with a WhatsApp CTA for B2B buyers
Consolidate the announcement bar to a single line: 'Free Shipping Above Rs. 999 | Same Day Dispatch' — remove app download from the sticky top bar
pdp.benchmark_tag: Standard — 9/10 electronics stores keep PDP first fold focused on product info

pdp.title: Product description is a wall of text without structured tabs
pdp.header: Tabbed product information improves PDP engagement by 20-30% — Robo Craze presents all product info as unstructured paragraphs
observations: Product description flows as a continuous block: bullet points followed by long paragraphs covering specs, features, compatibility, and use cases — no clear sections or tabs
The Raspberry Pi 4 PDP has: quick bullet points, a detailed description of 3+ paragraphs, key features list, a 'Note' section, and 'Why Buy' trust section — all in a single scroll
Technical products benefit from tabbed layouts: 'Overview | Specifications | Documentation | Reviews' — letting users jump to the information they need
Workshop promotions, course offers, and bulk order CTAs are interspersed with product info, creating a cluttered reading experience
recommendations: Implement tabbed product information: Tab 1 (Overview + spec icons), Tab 2 (Full Specifications table), Tab 3 (Documentation/Getting Started links), Tab 4 (Reviews) — the Ella theme supports this natively
Separate promotional content (workshops, courses, bulk orders) from product technical information — move promotions to a dedicated section below the product tabs
pdp.benchmark_tag: Standard — 8/10 electronics stores use tabbed product descriptions

pdp.title: Trust badges placed in sidebar, not near ATC button
pdp.header: Trust badges placed directly near the ATC button can boost conversion by 5-10% — Robo Craze's trust signals are in a separate sidebar, not where the purchase decision happens
observations: Trust badges ('Free shipping above Rs. 999', 'Technical Support', 'Same Day Shipping', 'Earn RC Coins', 'COD Available') are displayed in a 'Why Buy From Robocraze' section far below the ATC button
On mobile, these trust badges appear only after scrolling past the product description — most users making a purchase decision never scroll that far
Indian electronics benchmark standard (boAt, Croma) places a compact trust strip with delivery, warranty, and return icons directly below or beside the ATC button
The 'Raspberry Pi Approved Reseller' badge near the product image is good — but delivery and payment trust signals need to be near the purchase action
recommendations: Add a compact trust icon strip directly below the ATC button: Free Shipping | Authorized Seller | Easy Returns | COD Available — using small icons with short labels
Keep the detailed 'Why Buy From Robocraze' section lower on the page, but ensure the critical trust signals appear in the purchase decision zone
pdp.benchmark_tag: Growing — 7/10 Indian electronics stores place trust badges near ATC

cart.title: Cart drawer lacks product images and cross-sell
cart.header: A well-designed cart drawer with product thumbnails and cross-sell recommendations can increase AOV by 10-15% — Robo Craze's cart drawer is minimal
cart.benchmark_label: The Pi Hut — ATC Confirmation + Extras
observations: The cart drawer shows product name and price but the product thumbnail is partially visible and cut off by the drawer edge — making it hard to visually confirm the right product was added
No cross-sell or 'frequently bought together' recommendations appear in the cart drawer — missed opportunity to increase average order value
The Pi Hut shows 'Awesome Extras' cross-sell recommendations immediately after adding to cart, with product images, ratings, and ATC buttons for accessories
The 'Robocraze Academy' subscription offer (1 Month @ 199/-, 1 Year @ 999/-) in the cart drawer is not relevant to most product purchases and adds friction
recommendations: Redesign the cart drawer to show: clear product thumbnail, name, variant, price, and a 2-3 item 'Frequently Bought Together' carousel with complementary accessories
Replace the academy subscription offer with context-aware cross-sells: if adding a Raspberry Pi board, suggest cases, power supplies, and SD cards
cart.benchmark_tag: Standard — 7/10 electronics stores show cross-sell in cart drawer

cart.title: No coupon/discount code field visible on cart page
cart.header: A visible coupon code field on the cart page increases checkout intent — Robo Craze's cart page shows no discount code input before proceeding to checkout
observations: The cart page shows: product details, quantity, 'I need a GST invoice', In-Store Pickup option, Order Summary with Subtotal/Total, and PROCEED TO CHECKOUT — but no coupon/discount code field
Users who received the 'Spin to Win' coupon on arrival have no visible place to enter it until the checkout page — creating uncertainty about whether their discount will apply
The announcement bar says 'SAVE more when you BUY more. Upto 30% off on BULK PURCHASE' but there's no visible mechanism to apply this discount on the cart page
Adding a coupon field on the cart page creates a transparent pricing experience and reduces checkout abandonment from users unsure about discount application
recommendations: Add a collapsible 'Have a coupon code?' field in the Order Summary section — pre-populate it if the user received a code from the Spin to Win popup
Show active promotions (e.g., 'Add Rs. X more for free shipping' or 'Buy 2+ for 30% off') as dynamic messages above the checkout button
cart.benchmark_tag: Growing — 6/10 electronics stores show coupon field on cart page

cart.title: Fake urgency timer erodes trust
cart.header: Fabricated urgency tactics ('Someone ordered this — 09:49 minutes left') reduce trust with tech-savvy buyers — authenticity drives electronics conversions
cart.benchmark_label: The Pi Hut — Clean Cart Experience
observations: The cart page displays: 'Please, hurry! Someone has placed an order on one of the items you have in the cart. We'll keep it for you for 09:49 minutes' — this is a fabricated urgency message
Robo Craze's core audience — makers, engineers, students, and hobbyists — are technically literate and easily recognize fake urgency timers, which damages brand credibility
The Pi Hut uses genuine stock availability ('In stock') and real delivery estimates instead of artificial pressure tactics — building trust with the maker community
Electronics purchases are considered decisions, not impulse buys — fake urgency contradicts the buyer psychology of this category
recommendations: Replace the fake urgency timer with genuine stock-level indicators: 'Only 3 left in stock' (when actually low), or 'Order before 2 PM for same-day dispatch'
Use real value-add urgency: 'Free shipping on this order (above Rs. 999)' or 'Your items qualify for same-day dispatch from Bangalore warehouse'
cart.benchmark_tag: Differentiator — 8/10 top electronics stores avoid fake urgency timers

cart.title: No order savings summary on cart page
cart.header: Showing total savings reinforces purchase value and reduces cart abandonment by 5-8% — Robo Craze's cart shows no savings summary
cart.client_label: Robo Craze — Mobile Cart Order Summary
observations: The Order Summary shows only Subtotal (Rs. 12,856) and TOTAL (Rs. 12,856) — no savings line showing the total discount from MRP
The product line shows MRP Rs. 14,499 crossed out with sale price Rs. 12,856, but the order summary doesn't aggregate this into a 'Total Savings: Rs. 1,643' line
Indian e-commerce leaders (Croma, boAt) prominently display total savings in the cart order summary — reinforcing the value perception before checkout
Showing 'You Save Rs. 1,643 on this order' in the summary creates a positive reinforcement loop that reduces cart abandonment
recommendations: Add a green 'You Save Rs. X' line in the Order Summary between Subtotal and Total — dynamically calculating total savings from MRP across all cart items
When items qualify for free shipping (above Rs. 999), show 'Free Shipping: Saved Rs. 99' as an additional savings line for extra reinforcement
cart.benchmark_tag: Growing — 5/10 electronics stores show savings summary in cart
